concepts.title: What is a Mole?
concepts.content_html:
The Mole: Chemistry's Counting Unit

In chemistry, atoms and molecules are far too small to count individually. Instead, we use a special unit called the mole (abbreviated mol).

Avogadro's Number

One mole of anything contains exactly:

\[6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ particles}\]

This number is called Avogadro's number (\(N_A\)). Just as a "dozen" means 12, a "mole" means \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\).

Why Such a Big Number?

Atoms are incredibly tiny. One mole of carbon-12 atoms has a mass of exactly 12 grams — a quantity you can hold in your hand. But it contains 602,200,000,000,000,000,000,000 atoms!

Molar Mass

The molar mass of an element is the mass of one mole of its atoms, measured in grams per mole (g/mol). It equals the atomic mass from the periodic table with the unit changed from amu to g/mol.

  • Carbon: 12.01 g/mol
  • Oxygen: 16.00 g/mol
  • Hydrogen: 1.008 g/mol
  • Water (\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)): \(2(1.008) + 16.00 = 18.02\) g/mol

The Mole Triangle

Three key conversions connect moles, grams, and particles:

  • Moles to grams: multiply by molar mass
  • Grams to moles: divide by molar mass
  • Moles to particles: multiply by \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\)
  • Particles to moles: divide by \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\)
Key Takeaway: The mole is a bridge between the atomic world (atoms, molecules) and the measurable world (grams). Molar mass is the conversion factor between moles and grams.
Common Mistake: Don't confuse molar mass with atomic mass. Atomic mass is in amu (for one atom); molar mass is in g/mol (for one mole of atoms). The numbers are the same, but the units and scale are different!

problems.question_html: How many atoms are in 2 moles of carbon?
problems.question_raw: How many atoms are in 2 moles of carbon?

hints.text: One mole contains \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) particles. How many particles are in 2 moles?

problems.question_html: What is the molar mass of water (\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\))? Use H = 1.008 g/mol, O = 16.00 g/mol.
problems.question_raw: What is the molar mass of water (H2O)?

hints.text: Water has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom. Add up their molar masses.

problems.question_html: How many grams are in 3 moles of carbon? (Molar mass of C = 12.01 g/mol)
problems.question_raw: How many grams are in 3 moles of carbon?

hints.text: To convert moles to grams, multiply by the molar mass.

solution_steps.text: Use the formula: grams = moles \(\times\) molar mass.

problems.question_html: How many moles are in 44 grams of \(\text{CO}_2\)? (Molar mass of \(\text{CO}_2\) = 44.01 g/mol)
problems.question_raw: How many moles are in 44 grams of CO2?

hints.text: To convert grams to moles, divide by the molar mass.

problems.question_html: How many molecules are in 0.5 moles of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)?
problems.question_raw: How many molecules are in 0.5 moles of H2O?

hints.text: Multiply moles by Avogadro's number to get the number of molecules.

problems.question_html: A sample contains \(1.806 \times 10^{24}\) atoms of iron. How many moles of iron is this?
problems.question_raw: 1.806 x 10^24 atoms of iron = how many moles?

hints.text: Divide the number of atoms by Avogadro's number to get moles.

concepts.title: Molar Mass Calculations
concepts.content_html:
Calculating Molar Mass of Compounds

The molar mass of a compound is the sum of the molar masses of all atoms in its formula.

Step-by-Step Process

  1. Write the chemical formula
  2. Count the number of each type of atom
  3. Multiply each atom count by its atomic mass (from the periodic table)
  4. Add all the values together

Example: Glucose (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6\))

\[6(12.01) + 12(1.008) + 6(16.00)\]

\[= 72.06 + 12.10 + 96.00\]

\[= 180.16 \text{ g/mol}\]

Compounds with Polyatomic Ions

For compounds like \(\text{Ca(OH)}_2\), the subscript outside the parentheses multiplies everything inside:

\[\text{Ca(OH)}_2: 1 \text{ Ca} + 2 \text{ O} + 2 \text{ H}\]

\[= 40.08 + 2(16.00) + 2(1.008) = 40.08 + 32.00 + 2.016 = 74.10 \text{ g/mol}\]

Key Takeaway: Always count atoms carefully, especially with parentheses and subscripts. The molar mass is your key to converting between grams and moles.
Common Mistake: In \(\text{Ca(OH)}_2\), there are 2 oxygen atoms and 2 hydrogen atoms (the subscript 2 applies to everything inside the parentheses). Don't forget to multiply!

problems.question_html: Calculate the molar mass of \(\text{NaCl}\). (Na = 22.99, Cl = 35.45)
problems.question_raw: Molar mass of NaCl?

hints.text: NaCl has 1 sodium atom and 1 chlorine atom. Add their molar masses.

solution_steps.text: The molar mass of NaCl is 58.44 g/mol.

problems.question_html: Calculate the molar mass of sulfuric acid \(\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4\). (H = 1.008, S = 32.07, O = 16.00)
problems.question_raw: Molar mass of H2SO4?

hints.text: Count atoms: 2 H, 1 S, 4 O. Multiply each by its molar mass and add.

problems.question_html: What is the molar mass of calcium hydroxide \(\text{Ca(OH)}_2\)? (Ca = 40.08, O = 16.00, H = 1.008)
problems.question_raw: Molar mass of Ca(OH)2?

hints.text: The subscript 2 outside the parentheses multiplies both O and H: 1 Ca + 2 O + 2 H.

problems.question_html: How many grams are in 2.5 moles of \(\text{NaCl}\)? (Molar mass = 58.44 g/mol)
problems.question_raw: How many grams in 2.5 moles of NaCl?

problems.question_html: How many moles are in 90 grams of water? (Molar mass of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\) = 18.02 g/mol)
problems.question_raw: How many moles in 90 grams of water?

solution_steps.text: 90 grams of water is approximately 5 moles.

problems.question_html: Which compound has a greater molar mass: \(\text{CO}_2\) or \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)?
problems.question_raw: Which has greater molar mass: CO2 or H2O?

hints.text: Calculate or recall the molar mass of each compound.

concepts.title: Stoichiometry Basics
concepts.content_html:
Introduction to Stoichiometry

Stoichiometry is the study of the quantitative relationships between reactants and products in a chemical reaction. It lets you predict how much product you'll make or how much reactant you'll need.

Mole Ratios from Balanced Equations

A balanced chemical equation gives you the mole ratio between all species. For example:

\[2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\]

This tells us:

  • 2 moles of \(\text{H}_2\) react with 1 mole of \(\text{O}_2\)
  • 2 moles of \(\text{H}_2\) produce 2 moles of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)
  • 1 mole of \(\text{O}_2\) produces 2 moles of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\)

Mole-to-Mole Conversions

To convert between moles of different substances, use the mole ratio from the balanced equation as a conversion factor.

Example: How many moles of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\) are produced from 5 moles of \(\text{H}_2\)?

\[5 \text{ mol H}_2 \times \frac{2 \text{ mol H}_2\text{O}}{2 \text{ mol H}_2} = 5 \text{ mol H}_2\text{O}\]

The Stoichiometry Process

  1. Write and balance the chemical equation
  2. Convert given quantity to moles (if not already in moles)
  3. Use the mole ratio to convert to moles of the desired substance
  4. Convert to the desired unit (grams, particles, liters, etc.)
Key Takeaway: The coefficients in a balanced equation give you the mole ratio. This ratio is the bridge that lets you convert between any two substances in the reaction.
Common Mistake: Make sure the equation is balanced before using mole ratios! An unbalanced equation will give you wrong answers.

problems.question_html:
Given: \(2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\)

How many moles of \(\text{O}_2\) are needed to react with 6 moles of \(\text{H}_2\)?
problems.question_raw: How many moles of O2 needed for 6 moles of H2?

hints.text: The mole ratio of \(\text{H}_2\) to \(\text{O}_2\) is 2:1.

solution_steps.text: \(6 \text{ mol H}_2 \times \frac{1 \text{ mol O}_2}{2 \text{ mol H}_2} = 3 \text{ mol O}_2\).

problems.question_html:
Given: \(\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3\)

How many moles of \(\text{NH}_3\) are produced from 4 moles of \(\text{N}_2\)?
problems.question_raw: How many moles of NH3 from 4 moles of N2?

problems.question_html:
Given: \(2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}\)

If 3 moles of \(\text{MgO}\) are produced, how many moles of \(\text{Mg}\) were used?
problems.question_raw: 3 moles of MgO produced. How many moles of Mg used?

hints.text: The mole ratio of Mg to MgO is 2:2, which simplifies to 1:1.
If 3 moles of MgO are produced, 3 moles of Mg were used.

solution_steps.text: Ratio: 2 mol Mg produces 2 mol MgO, or 1:1.
\(3 \text{ mol MgO} \times \frac{2 \text{ mol Mg}}{2 \text{ mol MgO}} = 3 \text{ mol Mg}\).

problems.question_html:
Given: \(\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\)

How many moles of \(\text{H}_2\text{O}\) are produced when 5 moles of \(\text{CH}_4\) are burned?
problems.question_raw: Moles of H2O from 5 moles of CH4?

problems.question_html:
Given: \(\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3\)

How many moles of \(\text{H}_2\) are needed to produce 10 moles of \(\text{NH}_3\)?
problems.question_raw: How many moles of H2 to produce 10 moles of NH3?

solution_steps.text: \(10 \text{ mol NH}_3 \times \frac{3 \text{ mol H}_2}{2 \text{ mol NH}_3} = 15 \text{ mol H}_2\).

problems.question_html:
Given: \(4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\)

What is the mole ratio of iron to iron(III) oxide?
problems.question_raw: Mole ratio of Fe to Fe2O3?

hints.text: Read the coefficients directly from the balanced equation.
Fe has a coefficient of 4, \(\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\) has a coefficient of 2. The ratio is 4:2.

solution_steps.text: From the equation: 4 mol Fe produces 2 mol \(\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\).
The mole ratio is 4:2, which simplifies to 2:1.
For every 2 moles of Fe, 1 mole of \(\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3\) is produced.

problems.question_html:
Given: \(2\text{KClO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2\)

If 0.5 moles of \(\text{KClO}_3\) decompose, how many moles of \(\text{O}_2\) are produced?
problems.question_raw: 0.5 mol KClO3 decompose. Moles of O2 produced?

solution_steps.text: 0.75 moles of oxygen gas are produced.

problems.question_html:
Given: \(\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2\)

How many grams of \(\text{CO}_2\) are produced from 2 moles of \(\text{CaCO}_3\)? (Molar mass of \(\text{CO}_2\) = 44.01 g/mol)
problems.question_raw: Grams of CO2 from 2 moles of CaCO3?

hints.text: First find moles of \(\text{CO}_2\) using the mole ratio (1:1), then convert to grams.

solution_steps.text: Ratio: 1 mol \(\text{CaCO}_3\) : 1 mol \(\text{CO}_2\). So 2 mol produces 2 mol \(\text{CO}_2\).
